title: Jeremiah 16
subtitle: Forbidden to Marry, Mourn, or Feast

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1-13 — "Do Not Take a Wife or Have Sons or Daughters"

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah not to marry or have children in this place (v.2). The prohibition is a sign-act: Jeremiah's enforced celibacy embodies the coming catastrophe in which families will be destroyed. Marriage and children — the fundamental blessings of covenant life — are prohibited because normal life is about to end. The prophet's body becomes the message.

God also forbids Jeremiah from entering a "house of mourning" (bêt marzēaḥ, v.5) or a "house of feasting" (bêt mišteh, v.8). The marzēaḥ was a well-attested institution in the ancient Near East — a ritual banquet associated with mourning and ancestor cults. By prohibiting participation in both grief and celebration, God removes Jeremiah from the full range of communal life.

refs.note: Paul advises against marriage "because of the present crisis" — echoing Jeremiah's sign-act of celibacy as eschatological witness.
Ezekiel is forbidden to mourn his wife's death — a parallel sign-act where the prophet's personal loss embodies national catastrophe.
Jesus speaks of those who "have renounced marriage because of the kingdom of heaven" — voluntary celibacy as prophetic witness.

notes.note: The command not to marry is unprecedented in Israel. Marriage and procreation were covenant obligations (Gen 1:28). For a prophet to remain celibate was a living parable: the future has been canceled.
No mourning and no funeral meals. The dead will be so numerous that proper burial and grieving will be impossible. The normal social fabric of death rituals will collapse.

No feasting either. Joy itself will cease "before your eyes and in your days." The comprehensive prohibition — no marriage, no mourning, no feasting — eliminates every social context of human connection.

When the people ask "Why?" God answers with covenant logic: your ancestors forsook me, and you are worse than them. Therefore exile to an unknown land where you will serve other gods "day and night" — the ironic fulfillment of idolatry.

Calvin observes that celibacy here is not a superior spiritual state but a prophetic sign of judgment. The prohibition against marriage is a curse, not a blessing — it signals the end of normal life, not an elevation above it.
Calvin notes that the people are "worse than your ancestors" — each generation's sin compounds the previous one. Accumulated guilt makes judgment inevitable.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The marzēaḥ is attested in Ugaritic texts as a funerary banquet with ritual significance. The term appears also in Amos 6:7, where it describes the self-indulgent feasts of the wealthy.
The phrase "serve other gods day and night" contains bitter irony — in exile, the people will experience the full reality of the idolatry they chose freely in the land.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as a triple sign-act: no marriage (family collapse), no mourning (death too common for ritual), no feasting (joy extinguished). The three prohibitions systematically dismantle every dimension of social existence.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the celibacy command as the most extreme form of prophetic embodiment. The prophet does not merely announce the future — he lives it. His lonely, childless existence is a permanent, visible indictment of a society that has forfeited its future.

hist.context: Chapter 16 is the most personally costly oracle in Jeremiah. The prophet is commanded to forgo the three fundamental human activities: marriage (vv.1-4), mourning the dead (vv.5-7), and celebrating with the living (vv.8-9). Each prohibition is a sign-act forecasting total social collapse — there will be no families to raise, no funerals to attend (the dead will be too many), and no occasions for joy. The chapter anticipates Jesus's celibacy and Paul's counsel in 1 Corinthians 7.

sections.header: Verses 14-21 — A New Exodus and the Return

heb.paragraph: God will send "fishermen" and "hunters" (v.16) to pursue the scattered people. The image is ambiguous: fishing and hunting can signify either judgment (dragging people to exile) or restoration (gathering the dispersed). Jesus's call to "fish for people" (Matt 4:19) may deliberately evoke this Jeremiah passage, transforming judgment imagery into gospel invitation.

refs.note: Isaiah likewise declares: "Forget the former things; see, I am doing a new thing!" The new exodus tradition runs through both major prophets.
Jesus calls disciples to be "fishers of people" — possibly evoking Jeremiah's fishermen imagery in a restored, redemptive context.

notes.note: The new exodus promise: the return from Babylonian exile will be so dramatic that it will replace the Egyptian exodus as the defining redemptive event. This is fulfilled partially in 538 BC and fully in Christ.

Fishermen and hunters pursue the scattered people. Before restoration comes thorough judgment — God will repay "double for their wickedness" because they have defiled the land with "lifeless forms of their vile images."

A universalistic vision: "Nations will come to you from the ends of the earth." Even the Gentiles will acknowledge the futility of their inherited idols. This anticipates the gospel mission to the nations.

Calvin sees the new exodus as a prophetic principle: God's redemptive acts escalate throughout history. Each new deliverance surpasses the previous one, culminating in the ultimate redemption in Christ.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: This oracle is repeated almost verbatim in 23:7-8, forming an inclusio around the judgment oracles against the kings. The repetition frames the royal critique with redemptive hope.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies vv.14-15 as a "supersession oracle" — the new act of salvation supersedes but does not abolish the old. The exodus paradigm is not replaced but expanded to include a greater deliverance.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the nations'  coming confession as a vision of "epistemological revolution" — the nations discover that their inherited gods are "worthless idols." Truth breaks through ancestral deception.

hist.context: Verses 14-15 contain a remarkable promise: the exodus from Egypt will be superseded by a greater act of redemption. The people will no longer say "as the LORD lives who brought us up out of Egypt" but "who brought us up out of the land of the north." The return from exile will become the new defining act of God's salvation — a second exodus that eclipses the first.

themes.note: Prophecy and Judgment both at 9 — the sign-act genre and the comprehensive social collapse it symbolizes.

lit.note: The triple prohibition (no marriage, mourning, feasting) structures the first half; the new exodus promise provides unexpected hope after comprehensive negation.

The marzēaḥ was a well-attested institution in the ancient Near East — a ritual banquet associated with mourning and ancestor cults. ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Chapter 16 is the most personally costly oracle in Jeremiah…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Forbidden to Marry, Mourn, or Feast

translations.text: “They will die of deadly diseases. They will not be mourned or buried but will be like dung lying on the ground. They will perish by sword and famine, and their dead bodies will become food for the birds and the wild animals.”

They shall die of deadly diseases. They shall not be lamented, nor shall they be buried. They shall be as dung on the surface of the ground. They shall perish by the sword and by famine, and their dead bodies shall be food for the birds of the air and for the beasts of the earth.

For this is what the Lord says: “Do not enter a house where there is a funeral meal; do not go to mourn or show sympathy, because I have withdrawn my blessing, my love and my pity from this people,” declares the Lord.
“For thus says the LORD: Do not enter the house of mourning, or go to lament or grieve for them, for I have taken away my peace from this people, my steadfast love and mercy, declares the LORD.

thread.text: Jesus calls disciples to be "fishers of people" — possibly evoking Jeremiah's fishermen imagery in a restored, redemptive context.

coaching.tip: God forbids Jeremiah from marrying or having children (v. 2) — his celibacy is a prophetic sign that the next generation faces destruction. Unlike Isaiah's family (which became living prophecy), Jeremiah's singleness proclaims absence: no family because there's no future. The prophet's personal emptiness mirrors the nation's coming emptiness. Obedience costs him everything ordinary people take for granted.

'Men will no longer say, "As surely as the LORD lives, who brought the Israelites up out of Egypt," but "As surely as the LORD lives, who brought the Israelites up out of the land of the north"' (vv. 14–15). The return from exile will eclipse the Exodus as God's defining act. Jeremiah — like Isaiah 43:18 — envisions a salvation so great it recalibrates the entire tradition. The past miracle becomes the smaller one.
